data.title: BOJ holds rates at 0.75% as Middle East conflict fuels 2.8% inflation forecast
data.description:
The Bank of Japan maintained its short-term policy rate at 0.75% during its April 2026 meeting, though the decision saw a notable shift in board sentiment.

In a 6–3 vote, dissenters Hajime Takata, Naoki Tamura, and Junko Nakagawa broke from the majority to propose an immediate hike to 1.0%, citing upside risks to inflation from the Iran conflict.

Reflecting these pressures, the BOJ raised its FY2026 core inflation outlook to 2.8% (from 1.9%) and downgraded its growth forecast to 0.5%, as rising energy prices begin to weigh on the moderate recovery.

At the same time, policymakers said the FY2025 GDP projection was slightly lifted to 1.0% from 0.9%, lifted by last year’s trade deal with Washington.

data.title: Polaris beats Q1 estimates, reaffirms guidance
data.description:
• Polaris press release (PII): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.13 beats by $0.53.
  • Revenue of $1.66B (+7.8% Y/Y) beats by $20M.
  • First quarter reported diluted loss per share was $0.83• Primary factors affecting first quarter sales were higher shipments coupled with positive price and lowerpromotions.• First quarter market share gains in off-road vehicles (ORV) and snowmobiles.• Total Polaris retail sales for the quarter, excluding Youth, were up one percent versus last year. ORV retail saleswere up three percent, excluding Youth.• Polaris reaffirmed full year 2026 guidance provided on March 3, 2026.
  • The Company is reaffirming 2026 full year adjusted sales and adjusted EPS guidance. The Company expects 2026adjusted sales to be between $7.15 billion to $7.30 billion (vs. consensus of $7.29B) and adjusted EPS of $1.60 to $1.70 (vs. consensus of $1.67)

data.title: Tuesday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
FOMC Meeting Begins 

The Federal Open Market Committee meets eight times a year to determine the near-term direction of monetary policy. 

9:00 AM Case-Shiller Home Price Index

The S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller home price index tracks monthly changes in the value of residential real estate in 20 metropolitan regions across the nation. 

9:00 AM FHFA House Price Index

The Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA) House Price Index (HPI) covers single-family housing, using data provided by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.

10:00 AM Consumer Confidence

The Conference Board's confidence report surveys consumers on their assessments of the labor market, business activity, and their own financial conditions.

Confidence is expected to take a hit from worries about rising prices and uncertainty flowing from the Iran war. The consensus sees the index down at 89.4 in April from 91.8 in March.

10:00 AM Richmond Fed Manufacturing Index

This survey tracks business conditions in the Richmond Fed's manufacturing sector.

11:30 AM 2-Yr FRN Note Auction 

An FRN (floating rate note) is a security that has an interest payment that can change over time.

1:00 PM 7-Yr Note Auction

Treasury notes are sold at regularly scheduled public auctions. The competitive bids at these auctions determine the interest rate paid on each Treasury note issue.

1:00 PM Money Supply

The monetary aggregates are alternative measures of the money supply by degree of liquidity.

data.title: Simpson outlines 19.5%-20.5% 2026 operating margin as U.S. housing starts expected down low single digits
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Simpson Manufacturing Co., Inc. (SSD) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered net sales of $588 million, up 9.1% from the prior year quarter" (CEO, President & Director Michael Olosky), adding that growth was "primarily driven by our 2025 pricing actions" plus "foreign exchange," while "partially offset by an approximate 1% decline in volume as a result of softer housing start activity."
  • "The component manufacturer business delivered a strong quarter with volumes up double digits, driven primarily by new customer wins" (CEO Olosky), and management tied demand to "productivity-enhancing solutions" across "software, plates, equipment and design services" as truss manufacturers prioritize "labor efficiency, throughput and operational visibility."
  • "Our consolidated gross margin declined 130 basis points year-over-year to 45.2%" (CEO Olosky), attributing pressure to "higher material, factory and tooling and labor cost" and "start-up costs from the ongoing ramp of our Gallatin facility," while saying "our 2025 price increases, which we now expect will contribute approximately $130 million in annualized net sales helped offset these costs, including those attributable to tariffs."
  • "Pricing actions are contributing as expected and are projected to add roughly $130 million in annualized net sales, helping offset some tariff-related cost pressures" (CFO & Treasurer Matt Dunn), while also emphasizing, "we do not expect this level of revenue growth to carry through the remainder of the year."

Outlook

  • "We now believe 2026 housing starts in the United States will be down low single digits compared to 2025" (CEO Olosky), versus the prior quarter’s stance that housing starts would be "relatively flat year-over-year" (CEO Olosky, Q4 call).
  • "We continue to expect our consolidated operating margin to be in the range of 19.5% to 20.5%" (CFO Dunn), reiterating the same full-year range given last quarter (CFO Dunn, Q4 call).
  • "Additional key assumptions include... a lower overall gross margin based on imposed tariffs and increased depreciation costs" and "an expected $3 million to $5 million of footprint optimization costs in Europe" (CFO Dunn), plus "a projected $10 million to $12 million benefit on the sale of vacant land in the back half of 2026."
  • "We haven't taken any additional price increases in North America beyond the 2 that we announced last year" (CFO Dunn), while noting management may reassess if costs change: "potentially have to look at that if things change. But right now, nothing in the works."

Financial Results

  • "Net income totaled $88.2 million or $2.13 per fully diluted share" on "net sales" of "$588 million" (CFO Dunn).
  • "Consolidated gross profit increased 6.1% to $265.9 million, resulting in a gross margin of 45.2%" (CFO Dunn), and he quantified a key swing factor: "start-up costs in our Gallatin facility represented an approximate 100 basis point headwind to our first quarter gross margin."
  • "SG&A headcount was down approximately 9.1% year-over-year" and "operating expenses were 25.6%" of net sales (CFO Dunn), with "onetime costs in Q1 2026 of $2.3 million related to our strategic cost savings initiatives."
  • "We repurchased $50 million of our common stock" and ended the quarter with "a net debt position of $29.5 million" (CFO Dunn), while saying the company remains "focused on disciplined capital deployment" and "our commitment to return at least 35% of free cash flow to shareholders."

Q&A

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: "talk about the... cadence of demand and volumes in North America" after the "start of the war in Iran and spike in oil prices"; CEO Olosky: "it's been a bit of a soft selling season," and he said the company triangulated forecasts from "6, 7 different firms" plus customer checks.
  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: "Have you taken or contemplated any additional price increases"; CFO Dunn: "we haven't taken any additional price increases in North America" and management is "really focused on maintaining and kind of preserving our gross margins."
  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: land-sale timing; CFO Dunn: "It's definitely going to be in the back half... TBD, whether it's Q3 or Q4."
  • Trey Grooms, Stephens Inc., Research Division: end-market assumptions; CEO Olosky: repair and renovation "flattish to maybe up 1-ish percent," commercial "low single-digit range," and OEM "low single digits."
  • Kurt Yinger, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division: $130 million vs. $100 million pricing; CFO Dunn: "We're upping the annualized number to $130 million" from prior guidance and tied the change to "surcharges" and "price increases" in Europe plus North America mix toward "fasteners and anchors."
  • Timothy Wojs, Robert W. Baird & Co.: size of component manufacturing; CEO Olosky: "We have not commented on the size of the component manufacturing business."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with questions probing macro risk and cost pass-through, including "spike in oil prices" (Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.) and whether Europe could see "further risk" (W. Andrew Carter, Stifel).
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks but more cautious on forward demand, including "we do not anticipate sustaining the same level of revenue growth" (CEO Olosky) and "we do not expect this level of revenue growth to carry through" (CFO Dunn).
  • Versus last quarter: management language moved from "relatively flat" U.S. housing starts (CEO Olosky, Q4 call) to "down low single digits" (CEO Olosky), while keeping the operating margin range unchanged.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • "We now believe 2026 housing starts... will be down low single digits" (CEO Olosky) compared with the prior quarter’s "relatively flat" expectation (CEO Olosky, Q4 call).
  • "We now expect [2025 price increases] will contribute approximately $130 million in annualized net sales" (CEO Olosky) versus the prior quarter expectation of "at least $100 million" (CEO Olosky, Q4 call).
  • Analysts shifted toward near-term demand sensitivity and cost recovery mechanics (fuel, freight, tariffs, and Europe surcharges), while management repeatedly emphasized margin preservation and cost actions.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Near-term market conditions remain difficult" and the company "do[es] not anticipate sustaining the same level of revenue growth" (CEO Olosky), with housing starts expected to be "down low single digits" (CEO Olosky).
  • "Input cost headwinds" in Europe required "surcharges and price increases" (CEO Olosky), while in North America management highlighted monitoring costs like "fuel" and "potentially steel" without new price actions (CFO Dunn).
  • Margin headwinds included tariffs and ramp costs: "start-up costs in our Gallatin facility" (CFO Dunn) and "imposed tariffs" in the full-year framework (CFO Dunn).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 around price realization and cost actions offsetting tariffs and ramp costs, while emphasizing mix-driven growth areas such as component manufacturing software and OEM/mass timber. At the same time, leadership tempered expectations for the rest of 2026, updating U.S. housing starts to down low single digits and cautioning that Q1’s revenue growth rate is not expected to persist, while maintaining the full-year operating margin outlook of 19.5% to 20.5% and pointing to a back-half land-sale gain and ongoing capital returns, including buybacks under the $150 million authorization.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cadence forecasts 17% 2026 revenue growth, outlines $6.125B-$6.225B outlook as Hexagon integration weighs on margins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Cadence had a strong start to 2026 with accelerating AI demand and disciplined execution, delivering one of the best Q1s in company's history" and "Our record backlog of $8 billion was ahead of plan" (CEO, President & Director Anirudh Devgan).
  • "Given the accelerating momentum of our business, we are raising our 2026 revenue growth outlook to 17% and expect to achieve the Rule of 60 for the first time" (CEO Devgan).
  • "We announced a strategic collaboration with Google to optimize the ChipStack AI Super Agent with Gemini on Google Cloud" and "we announced an expanded partnership on AI and robotics with NVIDIA" (CEO Devgan).
  • "Our IP business continued its strong momentum, with 22% year-over-year revenue growth" and "Demand for our hardware accelerated in Q1, resulting in our best quarter ever" (CEO Devgan).
  • "Total revenue was $1.474 billion" and "GAAP EPS was $1.23, and non-GAAP EPS was $1.96" (Senior VP & CFO John Wall).

Outlook

  • "For our updated outlook for 2026, we expect revenue in the range of $6.125 billion to $6.225 billion" and "non-GAAP operating margin in the range of 43.5% to 44.5%" (CFO Wall).
  • "For Q2, we expect revenue in the range of $1.555 billion to $1.595 billion" and "non-GAAP EPS in the range of $2.02 to $2.08" (CFO Wall).
  • "The updated outlook" included "the Hexagon design and engineering business" and management said this was a change versus the prior-quarter outlook, which "does not include our pending acquisition of Hexagon's design and engineering business" (CFO Wall; CFO Wall in Q4 call).

Financial Results

  • "Robust design activity, coupled with our solid execution, drove 19% year-over-year revenue growth and 45% operating margin for Q1" (CFO Wall).
  • "Operating cash flow was $356 million" and "we used $200 million to repurchase Cadence shares" (CFO Wall).
  • "First quarter bookings were ahead of expectations" and "DSOs were 67 days" (CFO Wall).

Q&A

  • Yu Shi, Needham & Company: concerns about AI writing software and whether it threatens base-tool defensibility; CEO Devgan responded, "I'm very confident in our position in the base tool and our competitive advantage" and "I'm not worried that some other party will be able to write any better base tools."
  • Jason Celino, KeyBanc: asked why operating margin guide moved down; CFO Wall said it was "primarily the impact of including the Hexagon design and engineering business" and added, "We expect it to be dilutive to the tune of about $0.28" and "We'd expect it to be accretive in 2027."
  • Vivek Arya, BofA: asked how shortages and customer pricing power affect engagement; CEO Devgan said, "the environment is pretty healthy" and noted some customers may use "multiple foundries or nodes" which "would directly lead to more design activity for us."
  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: asked how agentic tools are priced and how Cadence captures value; CEO Devgan said new automation would be "priced as a subscription plus consumption model" and described agents running "10 or 100 variations" that drive more base-tool usage; CFO Wall added, "the overall pricing environment has improved" and "Pricing obviously remains value-based."
  • Sitikantha Panigrahi, Mizuho: asked on IP drivers and foundry contribution, and about EPS guide bridge; CFO Wall answered, "we did... raised by $0.08," while CEO Devgan highlighted portfolio expansion and said on Intel Foundry, "we are making very good progress with Intel, and we'll have -- soon, we'll have more to say."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to probing on defensibility, margins, and guide prudence, including questions about "defensibility" (Needham) and margin guide "coming down" (KeyBanc).
  • Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks and confident in Q&A, including "I'm very confident" (CEO Devgan) and "the overall pricing environment has improved" (CFO Wall).
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management kept a similarly confident tone on AI and workload expansion, while the current call added more integration-related explanations and near-term dilution detail tied to Hexagon.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Backlog increased to "$8 billion" in Q1 2026 from "$7.8 billion" exiting 2025 (CEO Devgan; CEO Devgan in Q4 call).
  • Guidance framing shifted: Q4 guidance explicitly said it "does not include" the Hexagon transaction, while Q1 guidance incorporated it and CFO Wall described 2026 as "an integration year" with near-term dilution.
  • AI narrative broadened from launching ChipStack in Q4 ("the world's first Agentic AI solution") to a broader "agentic full-flow platform" in Q1 with "AgentStack," "ViraStack," and "InnoStack" (CEO Devgan; CEO Devgan in Q4 call).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Our updated outlook... contains the usual assumption that export control regulations that exist today remain substantially similar for the remainder of the year" (CFO Wall).
  • On Hexagon integration, CFO Wall said, "2026 is an integration year" and cited acquisition dilution drivers including "lost interest income on the cash" and "acquisition-related integration costs," with mitigation implied in, "We'd expect it to be accretive in 2027."

Final Takeaway

Cadence management characterized Q1 as a "strong start" with "record backlog of $8 billion" and raised its 2026 revenue outlook to "approximately 17% year-over-year growth" while introducing a fuller agentic AI platform strategy and multiple partnership expansions. Management also emphasized that the updated 2026 outlook now includes Hexagon’s D&E business, which CFO John Wall said would be "dilutive to the tune of about $0.28" in 2026, with accretion expected in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: California fuel imports surge following refinery closures - Argus
data.description:
California's gasoline and jet fuel prices have jumped to multiyear highs, even as imports have soared, Argus reported Monday, as the state has become increasingly dependent on fuel imports following the closure of two large refineries.

California gasoline imports surged to 142K bbl/day in March, nearly triple the amount from a year earlier, after spiking to 122K bbl/day in February from 28K bbl/day, as Valero's 145K bbl/day Benicia refinery started to shut down, according to Argus, which cited data from Kpler.

Tightening regional supply and higher underlying Nymex RBOB values due to the Middle East war pushed prices higher, including Los Angeles regular CARBOB differentials that rose to a five-month high of $0.60/USG on April 21, more than $0.41/USG above pre-war levels, Argus said.

At the same time, in-state CARBOB production fell in mid-April, heightening supply constraints, as California refiners cut production of CARBOB gasoline 13% to 570K bbl/day, which Argus said was the lowest in at least five years.

Meanwhile, jet fuel prices on the west coast reached an all-time high of $4.92/USG on April 23, up from a previous high of $4.81/USG on March 20, according to Argus data; California jet fuel stocks had tumbled to a more than two-year low as of April 10.

South Korea—California's top supplier of jet fuel—relies heavily on the Middle East for its crude and naphtha supplies; Californian jet fuel arrivals from South Korea have averaged ~38K bbl/day so far in April, roughly even with March but down from 46K bbl/day in January-February, Argus reported.

ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UGA)
